replies.author: @b.w.22
replies.text:
And what’s more, they 100% were able to explore the very themes that “modern” writers say are so important to them. Janeway pretty clearly recognized that, as a woman, she was going to be more heavily scrutinized as a commander and that there may be moments of crisis where someone might try to challenge her. 

Exploring this over the first season and watching her assert the sort of command we associate with Starfleet captains in a vessel suddenly so very far from the “territorial waters” of the Federation, where challenge to command is a challenge to the authority of Starfleet generally. But when the “institution” of Starfleet is just this lone vessel, the authority of its captain is much more vulnerable, for instance, to the literal rebel Maquis who make up a decent part of the crew. So putting a character like Janeway in that, opposite Chakotay and B’elanna(?) Torres, is so perfect as it allows the actress and writers to highlight her strengths as a command figure different than, say, Riker. 

What made all of that work was the quality of the acting and the writers treating the character, captain of a Starfleet vessel, with respect and an understanding that her “bloodline,” as it were, traces back to Picard and Kirk and not to nurse Chapel or whatever.

replies.author: @ShaggyRogers1
replies.text:
​@johnsjohnson "just like in reality"

Except, that's NOT how it works in reality. The fact that you think it is just points out that you have no idea what you are even talking about at this point. 

The amount of training and education necessary to do all of the things handled by Scotty would mean that he would have aged out of the service before even stepping foot on a vessel to serve. 

The engineers in charge of the engine on a modern warship are not the same people repairing the weapons and maintaining the computer systems. The electricians ensuring that a vessel has power to critical systems are not the same people that are changing the oil on the engines. 

The more futuristic sci-fi you make a setting, the MORE complex infrastructure is needed to set the stage. A Star Trek warship would be even more complex and require even more specialists to operate. 

Even if there WAS such a savant that could handle everything, no military structure would allow such a large single point of failure on a vessel. What happens when Scotty is sick? All of those important tasks are now handed off to junior engineers that have zero experience handling emergency work?

Man, isn’t this true. And to the comment above saying that the writers feel us to be fans of Roddenberry and not Star Trek, I find that position pretty wild and had never heard it out that way. I mean sure, G.R. was an enormous force, but he wasn’t the single hand at the tiller: As I recall, the first film was considered “too Roddenberry” and that the Wrath of Khan was a product, in part, of different minds and perhaps even the influence of John Milius in the more explicit military symbolism throughout the production and naval combat between the Reliant and Enterprise.

So yeah, if it’s Roddenberry we like, there sure is a lot of variety to it all and many, many themes explored, such as affection between an android and human, the nature of consciousness and life, our duties to the less fortunate or advanced in the Prime Directive, international relations, service, sacrifice, family, living as “different” with Worf and the conflict of his identity as a Klingon with the values of the Federation and his duties as a junior Starfleet officer, and on and on to include Shakespeare and themes from Actuu and Moloda!

But sure - Trek is just some regressive, old patriarchal boolshi that’s out of touch with what the kids of the 28th century are really about. Or should I say the 67’th century???? Amirite bros???

Star Trek is a cult classic because basically everyone who watched it became fans of it.

I watched it pretty late in my life, starting with TOS around 2014 or so. And even with "outdated" VFX this show was amazing. Because a well written story beats expensive CGI every time.

And thats the only thing that modern "Star Trek" repeatedly fails to recapture (or even try to do so as it seems).

My favorite sci-fi shows are BSG, Star Trek, Stargate, Babylon. All of them have one thing in common, competent crew tossed into morally tough situations.

Modern Star Trek on the other hand is about incompetent people who struggle to deal with problems of their own creation.

Old sci-fi shows were about creating role models, "yeah, i want to be as this guy". New sci-fi seems to be about showing the worst of humanity so we would feel "right at home".

replies.author: @b.w.22
replies.text:
This is so well put. I think these writers fundamentally misunderstand the essence of “science fiction” and its appeal. Like all fiction, it is fundamentally imaginative and, in general, asks the reader/viewer to presuppose that whatever the setting is is possible. Oppose this to “fantasy” settings which are generally purely imaginative: One could have “one day, what if?” thoughts about the Federation, but can’t really do the same about Middle Earth, though this is in no way to discount fantasy.

Trek in particular really hinges on that sort of “one day, what if?” thinking, even as it has explored the conflicts of its day through the lens of “where no one has gone before.” The current writers think it’s valuable and interesting to “what if?” current zoomers into the Star Trek future, like the WB in space, “friend-zoned in the Neutral Zone” or whatever. That’s dumb; the Zoomers of today are that world’s distant past, like putting some zany Byzantine wiseacres into Top Gun.

What the best Sci-fi does is to “what if?” the _challenges_ of today into a future we might hope would be better able to cope with them, to imagine the very best of who we wish we might be grappling with problems that to us seem intractable. We want to admire our future selves - brave Starbuck, kind Troy, logical Spock, wise Sysco, and so on. 

Current Zoomers in the Star Trek future would be like those dunce aliens from TNG who act helpless to get better tech they don’t understand, given these “Academy” kids are eating coms badges and trying to lick chemicals and their instructors are sitting in chairs sideways.

author: @AlMcpherson79
text:
I have an opinion regarding Kurtzman Trek, for which I would get called Toxic. Guess what that opinion is, based on this basic opinion regarding the purpose of Starfleet and what that means for starship captains...
speaking as if I'm a Starfleet Captain, making an introduction speech at the first assembly for a group of new cadets:

In Peace, We are Explorers, Scientists, Engineers and Nurses.

From those who chart the stars, through those who figure out how spatial phenomena work, the people who fix the existing ships or design the next ones, and the healers who tend to us when we get sick or injured.

Except it's more than that.

We are Diplomats, meeting alien civilizations that have cultures, beliefs and attitudes that may be so antithetical to us yet we can't cry out "But thats wrong!" lest we get into another war like the Earth-Romulan War that killed trillions or the many many wars that resulted from that initial impression with the Klingons that even today we still don't have an accurate count for, not just the loss of so many starships and their crews, but the planets on both sides lost along the way. And eve those wars paled in comparison to the Alpha-Gamma Quadrant War, that almost saw the extinction of the Cardassian people at the hands of both those they had initially allied with, and the combined forces of the Federation, the Klingons, and the Romulans. And those three at that point weren't friends because of the previous wars that then required communication that fixed previous miscommunications, but in spite of it.

We may not just be studying something to learn, but because something is causing tremendous harm, a spatial phenomena that drifted into an inhabited star system, and we need to understand how it works and so how it's actually doing what it's doing to billions of people. And those people may not even be our people, or those of an ally.

The more energy you need, the more dangerous the process to get it, and the energy needed to bend space-time to allow non-relativistic travel between the stars is tremendous. One wrong move, and the starship you're on could literally blow up simply because of the energy release from the interaction of matter and anti-matter, and that's just for how Starfleet ships operate. Civilian ships may not use the same process to extract energy in useable form, I've certainly been passenger on some that didn't have a Matter Annihilation Reactor. But all forms that provide enough power to move hundreds of people and the starship they occupy from star system to star system are going to be very dangerous. And we need people who can fix that, maybe design new systems that make it safer. Or maybe you're the engineer sent onto an alien refugee ship whose crew was mostly killed by a spatial phenomena they didn't understand whilst running from an oppressive regime, no one remaining aboard knows how to fix the ship, and yet there's too many to bring aboard your own ship. But every moment you delay, more of those people will die from the phenomena, or at any moment their power core could explode as a result of important maintenance no longer being performed. and these systems would require a LOT of maintenance.

And it's not just ourselves that our healers treat, it's not just our citizens, either. All are welcome. So long as they're not waving a disrupter around threatening people. But even if they are... you're a doctor, do no harm. How are you going to react to an injured man from another culture, scared of the unfamiliar, brandishing a weapon out of fear of what he thinks you might be?

To become a Starfleet Officer, to wear that uniform, is to declare, You are the Best of the Best. That you will not insult that which you find different. You will not scoff at those who disagree with you. You will not shake in fear at the potential disaster awaiting you, and you will not bend nor bow to those trying to prevent you from carrying out your duty.

You all made it thus far to gain entry to the academy. Some would say that you have proven to be the Best. Here, we will make you even better than that, so that those here that graduate are the Best of the Best. And it is difficult. There is much to learn, under a lot of pressure, and perhaps, more than the typical officer experiences during most of their career. But we do those because you might be the atypical officer who would encounter more than your fair share of difficult situations.

Because there is one thing you need to know about Starfleet Officers. We are not what Breaks. When the Universe calls, and tells us, "you are done" we don't break, we don't blink. The Universe Does.

And if you're a starship captain, when you're on the bridge and the universe calls? Do you CRY? Do you protest that it isn't fair? When your crew looks to you, those less experienced than you to see if there's any hope? What will they take it to mean if you're crying? Despairing? When they look up, they need to believe that there's a way out of this, a way through. And they won't believe if their captain isn't showing that they believe it. There's a reason why the greatest of captains are said to actually have laughed in the face of danger. and It's not, just because some of them were crazy. Welcome to Starfleet.

author: @StarGeezerTim
text:
Not a fan? Let me haul out my shoebox of receipts, and see who the fan is, shall we?

Started watching the series in the 1960's' on our old Philips Black-and-White TV set. When it came to syndication, watched it every day after school. When the Saturday morning animated series hit in the early 70's, I watched that religiously. I built all the old AMT model kits. Read all the novelizations for the TV series. Owned the vinyl pressings of the shows. When the movies hit, I would watch them over and over until I knew each line by heart (and can still quote them all today.) Friends and I used to have all-day Trek marathon watch parties where we would watch them all back-to-back. When conventions started being a thing, we attended any that came within a couple hundred miles of here. I have framed autographed photos of Jimmy Doohan, Sir Patrick Stewart, a couple from Marina Sirtis (because daaayum) hanging on my walls in a place of honor. Owned and regularly listened to all the soundtracks from the movies and TNG that I could get my hands on. I think I earned my fanship credentials here. And what they're now laughably calling "Star Trek" is no more attached the show of my youth than a cat's yarked-up furball is to a tribble. 

Listen, I'm not telling folks they can't enjoy these new Star Trek treatments. Everyone deserves their own guilty pleasures, and if this is yours, enjoy it and don't let anyone talk you out of it. But by the same token, don't tell this 60+ year old lifelong fan of the property that because I don't sign off on these shows somehow invalidates decades of following the franchise. Shame on this "reporter" for the Mary Sue for even pursuing this line or reasoning. It is most illogical. 🖖🏼

author: @deadon4847
text:
Here is the full breakdown of exactly why this show is a disaster, and why the "review bombing" narrative is a total lie designed to protect mediocre writing. 🧵👇
​1. The Death of Meritocracy & The "Girl Boss" Archetype
​Starfleet used to be about the best of the best. Now? It’s a "Space Hogwarts" where biology and logic go to die.
​The "Genesis" Problem: We’re supposed to believe a 100lb girl who wakes up at 3:30 AM is effortlessly outpacing men twice her size in physical combat and speed? It’s not "empowering"; it’s a rejection of reality that makes the stakes feel fake.
​The "Incompetent Male" Trope: Look at Darrem. The second he gets authority, he becomes a bumbling imbecile who can't make a single decision. He eventually "submits" to Genesis, admitting she was "the right choice all along." This isn't writing; it's a lecture on why traditional masculinity is "obsolete."
​2. No Masculine Authority & The "Barefoot" Chancellor
​Where are the strong men? Where is the paternal wisdom of Picard or Sisko?
​Chancellor Ake is a Joke: She walks around BAREFOOT, slumps in every chair, and holds meetings in her PAJAMAS. This isn't "relatable"—it’s a total lack of respect for the uniform and the weight of authority.
​Neutered Mentors: Even legacy characters like The Doctor (Picardo) are being used for "poop jokes" and 32nd-century slang. They’ve taken a dignified medical icon and turned him into a "sanitized" accessory for a teen drama.
​3. Forced Inclusion & Lore Destruction
​The "inclusion" in this show is so forced it breaks the universe.
​Wheelchairs in the 32nd Century? In a world of transporters and bio-regenerators? It’s a 21st-century activist statement that makes zero sense in the lore.
​Klingon Erasure: They’ve turned the Klingons—a proud warrior race—into "sensitive," pacifist doctors who are interpretatively coded as gay just to check another box. They aren't expanding the lore; they’re vandalizing it.
​4. Atrocious Writing & The "Bathos" Trap
​The dialogue sounds like a 2026 Twitter feed. Every moment of tension is ruined by unfunny, cringey humor that never lands.
​No Stakes: When the climax of your episode is a "fungal prank war" between rival schools in the same building, you aren't watching Star Trek. You’re watching a Disney Channel original movie with a phaser skin.
​🛡 The REAL Problem: The "Shielding Protocol"
​The most toxic part? The people defending this.
When we point out that the writing is garbage and the characters are "neutered," the writers and cast immediately call us "bigots" and "racists." They hide behind the "Star Trek was always woke" lie to silence any critique of their ESG-driven activism.
​The Truth: Star Trek was about universal human values and merit. This show is about identity markers and trauma-dumping. If you have to attack your own fanbase to defend your show, you haven't made art—you've made a brochure.
​The critics are giving this a 87% while the fans are at 35%. That gap isn't "bigotry." It’s the sound of a legacy dying. 🖖📉
